statement.markdown: Given a set of distinct positive integers `nums`, return the largest subset of `nums` such that every pair of elements in this subset satisfies: `x` divides `y` or `y` divides `x`.

hints.text: The problem involves finding a subset of numbers where each pair has a divisibility relationship. Think about how you can use dynamic programming to build up a solution.
Sorting the input array `nums` could make it easier to find the divisibility relationships between numbers. Consider how you can use the sorted array to fill up a dynamic programming table.

For each number in the sorted array, you can find the largest divisible subset that ends with that number. Think about how you can use the previously computed values to efficiently compute the size of the largest divisible subset for each number.

editorial.title: Largest Divisible Subset
editorial.intuition: The problem requires finding the largest subset of a given array of numbers such that every pair of elements in the subset satisfies the condition that one is divisible by the other. The key observation here is that sorting the array helps in efficiently building up the subsets.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve generating all possible subsets of the given array and checking each subset to see if it satisfies the condition. However, this approach is extremely slow due to its exponential time complexity, making it impractical for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach involves using dynamic programming to build up the subsets in a bottom-up manner. By sorting the array first, we can ensure that when we are trying to extend a subset, we only need to consider the previous elements that are divisors of the current element.
editorial.optimalSolution: The dynamic programming approach has a time complexity of O(n^2) and a space complexity of O(n^2), where n is the length of the input array. This approach is much faster than the brute force approach for large inputs.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the dynamic programming approach can be proved by showing that it satisfies the optimal substructure property. Specifically, if a subset is optimal, then any subset of it must also be optimal. The approach ensures that we consider all possible subsets by iteratively extending the subsets in a bottom-up manner.
editorial.dryRun: For example, given the input [1, 2, 3, 4, 8], the dynamic programming approach would first sort the array to get [1, 2, 3, 4, 8]. Then, it would build up the subsets as follows: [1], [1, 2], [1, 3], [1, 2, 4], [1, 2, 4, 8]. The largest divisible subset is [1, 2, 4, 8].
commonMistakes: Not sorting the input array, which would make it difficult to efficiently build up the subsets.
Not using dynamic programming to store and reuse the subsets that have already been computed.
Not considering all possible subsets, which would lead to suboptimal solutions.
Incorrectly implementing the dynamic programming approach, which would lead to incorrect results.
Not handling the case where the input array is empty or contains only one element.
